articles.title: Tiny Teeth Tell the Story of Our First Primate Family
articles.summary: Scientists found tiny teeth fossils in Colorado that belong to Purgatorius, the earliest known relative of all primates, including humans. These teeth are smaller than a fingertip and were discovered in a new southern location, helping fill a gap in the fossil record. Purgatorius lived about 65.9 million years ago, right after the dinosaurs went extinct, and was a small mammal like a shrew that likely lived in trees. Before this, fossils were only found in Montana and Canada, so this discovery shows that primates might have spread southward from the north. The research team used a special washing process to find these small fossils, with help from students and volunteers. This finding helps us understand how our primate ancestors evolved and recovered after a big asteroid impact.

articles.title: Young Cancer Survivors Age Faster and Face Brain Challenges
articles.summary: Teens and young adults who beat cancer might age faster than others their age, according to new research. Scientists found that these survivors show signs of quicker aging in their cells and brains, affecting memory, attention, and thinking skills. The study, led by experts from the University of Rochester and St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, involved about 1,400 people who survived cancer for at least five years. Most had leukemia or lymphoma. Chemotherapy, a common cancer treatment, was linked to the fastest aging. Good news: healthy habits like exercise, quitting smoking, and eating well might help slow or reverse this aging. The goal is to help survivors live longer and better lives, as they often face challenges in school, work, and daily activities due to these issues.

articles.title: The Mystery of K'gari's Dried-Up Lakes
articles.summary: K'gari is the world's biggest sand island, and it has beautiful freshwater lakes. Scientists from the University of Adelaide studied old mud layers from these lakes and found something surprising. About 7,500 years ago, during a rainy time, some of the deepest lakes dried up. This is unusual because lakes usually dry when it's dry, not when it's wet. The researchers think wind patterns might have changed where the rain fell, so K'gari didn't get enough water even though it was raining nearby. This discovery helps us understand that K'gari's lakes could be at risk if the climate changes in the future. By looking at sediment, which is like a diary of the lake, scientists can learn about past events and protect these special places.

articles.title: Tiny Worms Thrive in the World's Driest Desert
articles.summary: Scientists discovered that tiny worms called nematodes are living in the Atacama Desert in Chile, which is one of the driest places on Earth. This desert has almost no rain, salty soil, and big temperature changes, making it very hard for life to survive. An international team from the University of Cologne studied these worms and found they are doing well in different parts of the desert. The worms help keep the soil healthy by controlling bacteria and recycling nutrients. The researchers looked at six areas, including sand dunes and salt flats, to see how the worms survive. They found that in higher places, many worms reproduce without a partner, which might help them live in extreme conditions. This study shows that even in very dry places, life can be strong and diverse, which is important for understanding how ecosystems work.

articles.title: Making New Homes for Butterflies in Glasgow
articles.summary: In Glasgow, a big project is starting to help butterflies and moths. People are creating 40 new wild spaces in parks, schools, and community places. This is because butterflies and moths are becoming fewer in the UK, and the project wants to stop this. A charity called Butterfly Conservation is working with Glasgow City Council to do this. They will plant wildflowers and make habitats where butterflies can live. Volunteers will help take care of these areas. The project costs £250,000 and is paid for by The National Lottery Heritage Fund and others. Kids in schools can join in too, and there are start-up packs with seeds for people to grow their own mini gardens. This helps nature and makes Glasgow a better place for everyone.

articles.title: Animals That Change Their Body Heat
articles.summary: Some animals can change their body temperature to survive tough times. Most mammals and birds keep a steady temperature, called homeothermy. But others, like the fat-tailed dwarf lemur, can change their temperature a lot in one day. This ability is called heterothermy. Scientists are learning that many animals use heterothermy to stay safe from dangers like storms, floods, and predators. For example, bats use torpor, a deep sleep, when it's cold or rainy to save energy. Sugar gliders also use torpor during big storms to stay warm in their nests. This helps them live through bad weather and find food later. Researchers use tiny devices to study these animals in the wild. By understanding heterothermy, we learn how animals adapt to their environments.
